summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Final hearing – Oral reasons for decision – All matters resolved by consent except father’s application for twice weekly facetime in addition to regular in person time – Electronic communication opposed by mother – Where clear history of family violence by father against mother – Where mother does not wish to have to engage with father - Where ICL proposes compromise once per fortnight communication - Where additional benefit of short facetime calls to children aged 3 and 5 is marginal in context of substantial in person time orders – Where requiring parents to speak regularly has a high risk of re-igniting conflict and of undermining agreement for children to spend regular time with father – Where high risk of re-igniting parental conflict outweighs marginal additional benefit of facetime communications. Consent orders made. No orders for electronic communication.

fulltext:
Knef & Darzi [2026] FedCFamC1F 154 (23 February 2026)

Source: https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/FedCFamC1F/2026/154.html

Knef & Darzi [2026] FedCFamC1F 154 (23 February 2026)

Last Updated: 8 May 2026

FEDERAL CIRCUIT AND FAMILY COURT OF AUSTRALIA

(DIVISION 1)

Knef & Darzi [2026] FedCFamC1F 154

File number(s):
	
NCC 1749 of 2024

	

Judgment of:
	
SMITH J

	

Date of judgment:
	
23 February 2026

	

Catchwords:
	
FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Final hearing – Oral reasons for decision – All matters resolved by consent except father’s application for twice weekly facetime in addition to regular in person time – Electronic communication opposed by mother – Where clear history of family violence by father against mother – Where mother does not wish to have to engage with father - Where ICL proposes compromise once per fortnight communication - Where additional benefit of short facetime calls to children aged 3 and 5 is marginal in context of substantial in person time orders – Where requiring parents to speak regularly has a high risk of re-igniting conflict and of undermining agreement for children to spend regular time with father – Where high risk of re-igniting parental conflict outweighs marginal additional benefit of facetime communications. Consent orders made. No orders for electronic communication.

	

Legislation:
	
Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) s 60CC

	

Division:
	
Division 1 First Instance

	

Number of paragraphs:
	
37

	

Place:
	
Newcastle

	

Date of hearing:
	
23 February 2026

	

Counsel for the Applicant:
	
Ms Dalrymple

	

Solicitor for the Applicant:
	
Joplin Lawyers

	

Counsel for the First Respondent:
	
Ms Thorne

	

Solicitor for the First Respondent:
	
Tasker and Johnston Lawyers

	

Solicitor for the Second Respondent:
	
The Respondent appeared in person

	

Solicitor for the Independent Children's Lawyer:
	
Mr Grooby

	

Solicitor for the Independent Children's Lawyer:
	
Legal Aid Newcastle NSW

ORDERS

	
NCC 1749 of 2024

FEDERAL CIRCUIT AND FAMILY COURT OF AUSTRALIA (DIVISION 1)

BETWEEN:
	
MS KNEF

Applicant

AND:
	
MR DARZI

First Respondent

MS OJALA

Second Respondent

	
INDEPENDENT CHILDREN'S LAWYER

ORDER MADE BY:
	
SMITH J

DATE OF ORDER:
	
23 FEBRUARY 2026

THE COURT ORDERS BY CONSENT THAT:

That all previous orders are discharged.
That the applicant mother, MS KNEF (born in 1999) (‘the mother’), shall have sole decision-making authority in respect of all decisions concerning the major long-term issues affecting the children, X born in 2020 and Y born in 2022 (Collectively referred to as “the children”).
That the mother will:
(a) Advise the father, MR DARZI (born in 1997) (‘the father’), in writing not less that fourteen days in advance of any decision on major long-term issues in relation to the children proposed to be made;

(b) Provide the father with all relevant information which is in the possession of the mother relating to the long-term issue;

(c) Provide any necessary authorities to enable the father to obtain any additional information from any relevant authority;

(d) The father is to provide the mother with any input which he wishes to express with respect to the decision to be made within 7 days of the date such notice was received from the mother, by way of the Appclose communication app;

(e) The mother is to consider the father’s input, if received within 7 days;

(f) The mother shall inform the father as to the decision made within 48 hours of making such decision.

That from the date of these orders the children shall live with the mother.
That from the date of these orders the children shall spend time with the father, as agreed in writing, but failing agreement, as follows:
(a) Commencing, Saturday, 7 March 2026 and Sunday, 8 March 2026 and continuing each alternate weekend for a period of 3 months, from 8am to 4pm on Saturday and from 9am to 3pm on Sunday;

(b) Following the conclusion of the period of time as provided for by Order 5 (a) and for a period of 12 months each alternate weekend on Saturday from 8am to Sunday at 3pm;

(c) Following the conclusion of the period of time as provided for by Order 5 (b) and continuing each alternate weekend Thereafter, each alternate weekend from after school (or 3pm if not a school day) on Friday to 3pm Sunday;

(d) Commencing Term 2 in 2028 and continuing during Term 3 2028 school holidays the children shall spend time with the father on the first weekend of the term 2 and 3 school holidays from after school (or 3pm if not a school day) on Friday until 3pm on Monday;

(e) Commencing Term 4 in 2028 and continuing each year thereafter, the children shall spend time with the father during the term 4 school holidays on the third weekend and fifth weekend of the holiday period from 3pm on Friday until 3pm on Tuesday.

(f) Commencing Term 1 in 2029 and continuing during the Term 1, 2 and 3 school holidays each year thereafter, the children shall spend time with the father during the term 1,2 and 3 short school holidays on the first weekend of those holidays from after school (or 3pm if not a school day) on Friday until 3pm on Tuesday.

(g) On each of the children’s birthdays providing the father is not already spending time with the children, if the birthday falls on a school day, the time is to occur from conclusion of school until 4.30pm and if the birthday falls on a non-school day the time is to occur from 10am to 2pm.

(h) For Father’s Day from 5pm on the Saturday before Fathers’ Day until 3pm on Fathers’ Day.

Notwithstanding any other order, the children shall spend time with the father from 2pm on Christmas Day to 4pm Boxing Day in even numbered years and from 2pm on Christmas Eve to 2pm on Christmas Day on odd numbered years.
Notwithstanding any other order the father’s time with the children shall be suspended on the following occasions:
(a) On the Mother’s Day weekend from 5pm on the Saturday before Mother’s Day until 3pm on Mother’s Day;

(b) For Christmas in even numbered years from 2pm on Christmas Eve until 2pm on Christmas Day; and

(c) For Christmas in odd numbered years from 2pm on Christmas Day to 4pm Boxing Day.

That for the purpose of the operation of order 5 the father’s time in order 5 c) shall be suspended during the holiday time provided for in orders 5 d) – f).
That for the purposes of order 5-7, changeover shall occur at Suburb C McDonalds at the start and finish of the children’s time with the father unless otherwise agreed in writing between the parents.
That the children shall spend time with the maternal grandmother, MS OJALA (born in 1970) (‘the maternal grandmother’) as agreed between the mother and maternal grandmother.

Medical

That the mother is to keep the father informed of all medical practitioners and/or allied health practitioners engaged with the care of the children including contact numbers and email addresses.
Each parent shall notify the other of any medical emergency, illness or injury suffered by the either child whilst in their respective care warranting treatment by a third party and shall authorise any treating health professionals to communicate with the other parent about the condition and treatment of the child.
That if any of the children take prescribed medication, the parent who buys the children’s medication will provide the other parent with the medication and instructions for its use, when the child goes into the other parent’s care.

Education, Health or Welfare Information

Subject to the directions of the organisations, this Order shall constitute full authority for the mother and father to obtain directly from any educational, health or welfare agency or professional involved in the care of the children all information regarding the education, health and welfare of the children and for this purpose each of the parents shall keep the other informed of the names and contact details of any education, health or welfare agency or professional so involved in the welfare of the children.
That the father is at liberty to attend all school events to which parents are ordinarily invited.

Restraints

The parties are each restrained by injunction from denigrating one another or members of one another’s household or family in the presence of or within hearing of the children and will remove the children from the presence of any third party doing so.
The parties are each restrained from knowingly exposing the children to any form of ‘family violence’ as that term is defined in s4AB of the Family Law Act.
The parties are each restrained from physically disciplining the children or permitting a third party to physically discipline to the children.

AppClose Parenting App

The parents shall communicate with each other via the co-parenting app known as AppClose or any other co-parenting communication app as agreed in writing between the parents in relation to the care, welfare and development of the children and all issues regarding the children.
The parents will input all relevant information in relation to medical, education and extracurricular activities into AppClose within forty-eight (48) hours of making any such appointments or becoming aware of any events relating to the children.

Note: The form of the order is subject to the entry in the Court’s records.

Note: This copy of the Court’s Reasons for judgment may be subject to review to remedy minor typographical or grammatical errors (r 10.14(b) Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth)), or to record a variation to the order pursuant to r 10.13 Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).

Part XIVB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) makes it an offence, except in very limited circumstances, to publish an account of proceedings that identify persons, associated persons, or witnesses involved in family law proceedings.

IT IS NOTED that publication of this judgment by this Court under the pseudonym Knef & Darzi has been approved pursuant to subsection 114Q(2) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).

ORDER EX TEMPORE

SMITH J

These are oral reasons for decision in a final hearing in parenting proceedings to determine the parenting orders pursuant to Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 of the Commonwealth (‘the Act’) as currently in force, which are in the best interests of the subject children, X, (born in 2020) aged five, and Y, (born in 2022), aged three, jointly (‘the children’).
The applicant mother is Ms Knef, (born in 1999) aged 27, and the first respondent father is Mr Darzi, born in 1997) aged, 28. The second respondent is the maternal grandmother, Ms Ojala, (born in 1970) aged 56.
The children currently live with the maternal grandmother and spend time with the father and supervised time with the mother in accordance with interim orders dated 9 April 2025.
The matter was listed before me for final hearing today. The father and mother were both legally represented, including by counsel. The maternal grandmother was self-represented, but has had the benefit of speaking with the duty solicitor. An independent children's lawyer appeared with a solicitor advocate, representing the interest of the children.
I have had the benefit of reading the filed material of the parties, including case outlines, affidavits, and other material as marked in the exhibit sheet, and in particular, the Child Impact Report and Family Report of the Court Child Expert, Ms B, dated 2 September 2024 and 13 February 2025, respectively.
Across the course of the day, the parties, including the ICL, have come to what I consider to be an eminently sensible resolution of almost the entire proceedings. [1]
Those orders are broadly consistent with the recommendations of the Court Child Expert, where I am satisfied on the evidence before me, although not tested, that the evidence does not establish that the mother currently poses any unacceptable risk of harm to the children, and that she has addressed the issues which led to the children's removal.
I have indicated that I would make, and I do make, the proposed orders by consent, which will be published with this short judgment. These include that the children live with the mother, who shall have sole decision making authority with consultation, and spend time with the father as agreed.
There is one outstanding issue requiring determination, which is the father's application for twice-weekly FaceTime and telephone calls in similar, although not identical, terms to that set out at paragraph 6 of his immediate response.
In making these consent orders I accept that the evidence indicates that the father does, as noted in the family report at paragraph [12], appear to have substantially dealt with and stabilised his mental health issues.
I am reasonably satisfied, as the parties implicitly agree by the orders they propose, that he does not presently represent or pose an unacceptable risk of harm of unsupervised time with the children, although I note there are still some concerns about his general parenting capacity and understanding of the impacts of family violence, and these issues are relevant to my determination of the one matter which is outstanding, which I will come to.
I refer, in particular, to the comments reported by the Court Child Expert as having been made by the father at, for example, paragraph [36] of that report, where the father accepted he had attended the mother's home in November 2023, and that he gave her the impression he was going to commit suicide, but told the Court Child Expert that he did not consider this to be abusive.
Similarly, the father said that he indicated he was to attempt suicide in August 2023 when picking up a knife in the mother’s presence. He disputed whether he waved the knife at the mother as she alleged. However, even on the agreed facts the father said that in his view this event did not constitute family violence.
Also troubling, on the issue of the father’s general parenting capacity, was his denial, recorded at paragraph [48] of that report, that he had learned anything from the Triple P parenting course he attended.
I further note the issues raised concerning his mental health raised at paragraphs [51] to [54], and in particular, the summary at paragraph [57]:
Whilst Mr [Darzi] presented as committed to the children, he appeared to have limited insight as to the impact that his behaviour may have had on their children during their early years. In this regard, Mr [Darzi] seemed dismissive in respect of his mental health concerns, and attributed all associated behaviour to not having received a diagnosis of ADHD at the time, and not being prescribed the appropriate medication. There is thus a concern that Mr [Darzi] presented as passive regarding his actions, with a lack of full accountability or responsibility for his behaviour.
I also note the expert's comments at paragraph [74] and [75], as raised in submissions by the ICL. I also note the concerns the Child Court Expert raised in the last sentence of paragraph [82]:
There is thus a concern about Mr [Darzi’s] lack of understanding of family violence and the potential impact on children, and about him engaging in similar behaviour in the future.
It is in this context that I consider the one outstanding issue which was not able to be resolved, and which was appropriately, by consent, dealt with by way of addresses without the need for cross-examination. Cross-examination would have added nothing to my determination.
In relation to twice-weekly FaceTime and telephone calls the father submitted, in substance, noting I was taken to the relevant paragraphs of his and the mother's affidavit, that there were prior electronic communications and that, except for a small issue with the mother saying she felt criticised, it had gone well. It had only stopped due to the circumstances of the ADVO against him which I do not need to address on this topic.
Given the children's young age, and the importance of consistency of contact, he says that it would be in the children's best interest to have this regular contact.
It was also submitted for the father that this would allow the father to have regular contact with the children as a protective factor, given the history which resulted in the children being removed from the mother's care.
On that last point, noting the involvement of the maternal grandmother and that there are some child care workers involved, and that this would be likely a very short telephone call monitored by the mother, I did not give any weight to that last argument concerning any additional protection for the children, particularly where there is consent that they live with her.
The mother and the ICL oppose the father's application. The mother says that her well-being should be protected by limiting the extent of her contact required with the father in the context of her concerns about family violence towards her, and noting the contents of the Family Report which I have referred to.
The mother raised X's medical issues on the paediatrician's report as a factor against requiring her to attend to phone calls, and I note the Court Child Expert identified clear changes in X’s behaviour across the course of the day in circumstances where she did not have her medication, making it clear that she does have issues with her attention span and behaviour.
Obviously, the limited attention span of Y at three years of age is also an issue.
The mother submitted that these telephone calls would add little, if any, additional benefit to the children in terms of maintaining a relationship with the father, where they will be spending relatively significant in-person time with him on a regular basis across a long period. On the other hand, by requiring the parents to communicate in person regularly they create a real risk of conflict between the parents, which could undermine their current agreement, and that this risk of conflict significantly outweighs any small additional potential benefit to the children.
The mother submitted that the application was about the father's emotional need to communicate with the children, and not about the children's needs and best interests, and therefore, given the risk that this required contact could send the current very sensible resolution off the rails, was not in the children's best interest.
The ICL took a similar position to the mother on the issue, although proposing in her final handwritten proposed Orders[2] that if either of the children asked the mother to speak with the father, she would be required to contact the father via SMS, and then confirm a time between 4.30pm and 6.30pm on that day, or the following day, for the father to initiate a FaceTime call with the children.
Although I raised with the parties, and did consider a middle position of once a week mandatory communication in the middle of the period between in-person time, having regard to the statutory provisions of section 60CC, and noting there are no real safety issues for the children involved in the telecommunication, and noting there is no expert evidence on the issue, on balance, I accept the mother’s submission and find that the children's best interests are served by minimising the extent to which the father and mother are required to interact.
It seems to me that any telephone or FaceTime calls would be unlikely to last for more than a few minutes, given what we know about the children's age and X's attention span, and that this would be of nominal, if any, additional benefit to the children.
Whereas, on the other hand, given the history between the parties and given my concerns which arise from the experts' report about the extent to which the father does genuinely acknowledge and accept the extent of his prior conduct as family violence, and in particular its effect on the mother, it seems to me that requiring the parties to speak a couple of times a week, or even once a fortnight, creates a significant risk that the parents would again come into conflict, and that this would undermine the present agreement which the parties have, as I've said, see the children spending real and significant in-person time with the father over the long term.
It is this in-person time between the father and the children that will build and maintain the relationship and short telephone or FaceTime calls will add little.
I accept the father wants to maximise his relationship with the children, but where the additional telephone calls or FaceTime for a few minutes here or there a couple of times a week would, in my view, add little, if anything, to the relationship, and where I think it creates a significant risk that the parties would come into conflict, and so put at risk the important long-term in-person time, I do not consider the father’s proposal is in the best interests of the children.
I did consider the ICL's proposal, and whilst initially attractive as a compromise, it still seems to me to create risks of further litigation. If, for example, the father says innocently to the children, "Oh, I wish I had a chance to speak with you," and whether or not they have actually said anything to the mother they respond, as children often will, saying what they the father to hear that eg "we asked mummy to speak with you”, whether they in fact had or had not, it gives rise to the risk of litigation, and it gives rise to the risk of conflict between the parents, and so it gives rise to risk that the important long-term operation of the orders for in-person time will be at risk because of conflict between the parties.
In those circumstances, for these reasons, I find that the risk of the parties coming into conflict outweighs what I consider the relatively nominal benefit to the children of such an order.
Obviously, the mother, as the person exercising primary decision-making, with whom the children will be living, would be at liberty to arrange with the father for telephone or FaceTime calls if the children ask her to, but I think the best course is that as the children age, if they ask her to, that she should consider meeting their request, but there should be no order requiring communication either as proposed by the father nor as proposed by the ICL.
In those circumstances, the orders will be the consent orders which I have approved and there will be no additional orders.
Those are my reasons.
